county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Butler, Coffee, Covington, Lowndes, and Pike counties. County seat Luverne. South-central Alabama agricultural county. Central of Georgia Railway stop. Five parent counties required for pre-1866 research.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Luverne. Created in 1866 from Butler, Coffee, Covington, Lowndes, and Pike — check all five parent counties for earlier records. Crenshaw County records are intact from 1866. The county is in south-central Alabama with mixed agriculture (cotton and peanuts). The Crenshaw County Historical Society has local family files. Luverne was a stop on the Central of Georgia Railway. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations are important supplements. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records. Confederate soldier records from multiple parent counties may document Crenshaw County families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Crenshaw County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1877 from Blount, Morgan, and Winston counties. County seat Cullman. Unique German immigrant colony founded by Colonel John Cullman in 1873. German-language church records and immigrant naturalization records. Ave Maria Grotto and St. Bernard Abbey.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Cullman. Created in 1877 from Blount, Morgan, and Winston — the county is relatively late. Colonel John Cullman founded the city as a German immigrant colony in 1873 — this gives the county a unique ethnic heritage with German-language church records, German-American fraternal organizations, and immigrant naturalization records. Check naturalization records at the Cullman County Courthouse and NARA for German immigrants. The Ave Maria Grotto and St. Bernard Abbey have Catholic records. The Cullman County Museum has strong German-American family history collections. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriage records. For pre-1877 records, check Blount, Morgan, and Winston counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Cullman County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1872 fire destroyed many early records at courthouse in Newton
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1824 from Covington and Henry counties. County seat Ozark (since 1870, previously Newton). 1872 courthouse fire at Newton destroyed many early records. Wiregrass region. Fort Rucker (now Fort Novosel) Army aviation center since 1942.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Ozark. The 1872 fire at the courthouse in Newton (original county seat) destroyed many early records — this is a burned county. The county seat moved to Ozark in 1870. Dale County was formed in 1824 from Covington and Henry. Fort Rucker (now Fort Novosel), the Army aviation center, has been in Dale County since 1942 — military family records through NPRC. The county's Wiregrass region had distinctive settlement patterns. The Dale County Historical Society in Ozark has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. For pre-1824 records, check Covington and Henry counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Dale County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Montgomery County. County seat Selma. One of the wealthiest antebellum cotton counties. Selma was a major Confederate arsenal. Site of the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery voting rights marches. Old Cahawba (first permanent state capital 1820-1826) is in the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Selma. Dallas County records are substantially intact from 1818. The county was one of the wealthiest antebellum cotton counties with massive enslaved populations. Selma was a major Confederate arsenal and manufacturing center — check Confederate industrial records at NARA. The Selma to Montgomery marches (1965) were pivotal civil rights events — related records at the National Voting Rights Museum in Selma. For African American research, Dallas County has extensive slaveholder records, estate inventories, and Freedmen's Bureau records. The Old Cahawba Archaeological Park preserves Alabama's first permanent state capital site (1820-1826). FamilySearch has microfilmed deed books, probate records, and marriages. ADAH holds Dallas County tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Dallas County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1876 courthouse fire destroyed many records; additional loss of older records when county seat moved from Lebanon
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1836 from Cherokee Cession. County seat Fort Payne. 1876 courthouse fire; additional record losses when county seat moved from Lebanon. Appalachian northeast Alabama. Fort Payne was a Cherokee removal assembly point.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Fort Payne. The 1876 courthouse fire destroyed many records, and additional older records were lost when the county seat moved from Lebanon — this is a burned county. DeKalb County was formed from Cherokee Cession in 1836. The county is in the Appalachian northeast corner of Alabama with strong Tennessee and Georgia family connections. Fort Payne was a Cherokee removal site — check removal records at NARA. The county had Unionist sympathizers during the Civil War. The DeKalb County Historical Society has local files. Lookout Mountain and Sand Mountain communities had distinctive settlement patterns. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for DeKalb County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Autauga, Coosa, Montgomery, and Tallapoosa counties. County seat Wetumpka (briefly Alabama's capital in 1826). Coosa River runs through the county. Near Horseshoe Bend battlesite.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Wetumpka. Created in 1866 from Autauga, Coosa, Montgomery, and Tallapoosa — check parent counties for earlier records. Wetumpka was briefly the state capital of Alabama (1826). The Coosa River runs through the county, and early mill and ferry records document riverside families. The county includes the site of the last battle of the Creek War (1814) at Horseshoe Bend (in adjacent Tallapoosa County). Elmore County records are intact from 1866. The Elmore County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriage records. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Elmore County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1868 from Baldwin and Conecuh counties. County seat Brewton. Conecuh River valley. Timber and turpentine industry (T.R. Miller Mill Company). Poarch Band of Creek Indians has members in the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Brewton. Created in 1868 from Baldwin and Conecuh — check parent counties for earlier records. Escambia County is in the Conecuh River valley with timber and turpentine as major early industries. The Poarch Band of Creek Indians has members in Escambia County — check tribal enrollment records for Native American ancestry. The T.R. Miller Mill Company records document lumber industry families. Atmore, the county's largest city, was a railroad town. The Escambia County Historical Society has local family files. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds and marriages. For Conecuh County parent records, note that the 1866 fire destroyed most of them.

county_metadata.parent_county: Blount, Calhoun, Cherokee, DeKalb, Marshall, St. Clair
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Blount, Calhoun, Cherokee, DeKalb, Marshall, and St. Clair as Baine County; renamed Etowah 1868. County seat Gadsden. Iron and steel manufacturing center. Six parent counties for pre-1866 research.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Gadsden. Created in 1866 from Blount, Calhoun, Cherokee, DeKalb, Marshall, and St. Clair — this is an unusually large number of parent counties. Originally named Baine County, renamed Etowah in 1868. Gadsden became a major iron and steel manufacturing center in the 1880s. The Etowah County Historical Society has industrial and family records. Goodyear Tire and Republic Steel employment records document worker families. Noccalula Falls area was significant for Cherokee history. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages from 1866. For earlier records, check all six parent counties. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Etowah County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1824 from Marion, Pickens, and Tuscaloosa counties. County seat Fayette. 1866 courthouse fire destroyed many records. Unionist leanings during Civil War. Economy based on farming, timber, and coal mining.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Fayette. The 1866 courthouse fire destroyed many records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for microfilm copies and state-level duplicates. Fayette County was formed in 1824 from Marion, Pickens, and Tuscaloosa. The county had Unionist leanings during the Civil War, similar to other north Alabama hill counties. The county's economy was based on small farming, timber, and coal mining. The Fayette County Historical Society has local materials. Beverly is the county's oldest community. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from the 1866 restart. For pre-1824 records, check the three parent counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Fayette County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Fayette County from 1866; Probate Judge office in Fayette maintains originals; ADAH holds microfilm copies of historical deed books
source_profiles.survival_notes: Burned county: 1866 courthouse fire destroyed many records

source_profiles.repository: Fayette County Probate Judge, Fayette / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and bonds for Fayette County from 1866; Probate Judge issues marriage licenses in Alabama; FamilySearch has indexed many Alabama marriages

source_profiles.source_name: Fayette County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Fayette County; Alabama first enumerated in 1820 federal census; slave schedules in 1850 and 1860; agricultural and manufacturing schedules available

source_profiles.source_name: Fayette County Civil War Military Records -- ADAH / NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History / NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Both Confederate and Union service records for Fayette County residents; Union Alabama regiments (1st Alabama Cavalry, USA and others) recruited from north Alabama hill counties; Confederate service compiled from ADAH muster rolls; pension records at ADAH (Confederate) and NARA (Union)

source_profiles.source_name: Fayette County Federal Land Patents -- BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Fayette County from the General Land Office; Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government; patents searchable and downloadable at BLM GLO website

source_profiles.source_name: Fayette County Church and Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: ADAH / FamilySearch / Local churches and cemeteries
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian) for Fayette County; cemetery transcriptions at Find A Grave and USGenWeb; ADAH has some microfilmed church records; essential for vital events before state registration (1908)

county_metadata.record_losses: 1890 courthouse fire destroyed many records; earlier fire in 1864 during Civil War
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Russellville. Two courthouse fires (1864 Civil War, 1890) destroyed many records. Bear Creek and Cedar Creek settlement areas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Russellville. Two courthouse fires (1864 during the Civil War and 1890) destroyed many records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH for microfilm copies and state-level duplicates. Franklin County was formed in 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession lands, making it one of Alabama's original counties. The Bear Creek valley and the Cedar Creek settlement attracted early families from Tennessee and the Carolinas. Russellville was a significant antebellum town. The Franklin County Historical Society maintains local research files. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. ADAH holds Franklin County tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Franklin County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants
